responseSet.title: Reply to Commentaries by Kindler and Shapiro
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Commentaries by Kindler and Shapiro

Ruth Gruenthal MSS

Ruth Gruenthal

300 West End Avenue
New York, NY 10023

Ruth Gruenthal  

Kindler and Shapiro undertake the complex task of commenting on two quite differently framed papers. Moreover, the authors hold distinct theoretical perspectives. Kindler looks at both papers in the light of a personal journey from orthopedic surgeon to analyst informed by self psychology. He reflects on the way both papers pose challenges to a version of himself that he once sought to maintain. For Kindler the issues raised in each paper suggest that we are in “a new era of psychoanalytic practice.” Shapiro considers each paper from the vantage point of gender issues and the use of a two-person psychology. Although her discussion raises salient theoretical and clinical issues, Shapiro's commentary does not address the thesis of my paper. Rather, she focuses almost exclusively on the clinical vignette illustrating my position.

Shapiro suggests that I am hiding my preconceptions about gender behind theory. In my view, I am arguing that theory organizes listening and understanding relevant to gender. Shapiro proposes that, in common with Hirsch, I hold an essentialist, dichotomous, stereotypical, commonsense, and conformist view of gender. Preconceptions about gender do, of course, pervade analysts' personal and theoretical perspectives. I concur with Shapiro's emphasis on the need for a critical examination of conscious, as well as unconscious, psychological and cultural “belief systems” about gender. Shapiro's conceptual agenda skews her understanding of my clinical material. She notes that my finding of Mr. X's cross-gender transference (seeing me in the paternal transference) goes counter to a traditional view of equating gender with anatomy. Yet Shapiro sets aside this contradiction with a multilayered argument. She first asserts that I am defending against intimacy and gender fluidity, establishing these as her key principles. Then shifting ground, Shapiro points to the influence of my countertransferential resistance against “seeing myself as the sickly, spacey mother.

responseSet.title: A Self-Psychological Perspective on Shabad's “Resentment, Indignation, Entitlement”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Self-Psychological Perspective on Shabad's “Resentment, Indignation, Entitlement”

Frank Lachmann PHD

Frank Lachmann

393 West End Avenue, #1-A
New York, NY 10024

Frank Lachmann  

In an attempt to investigate a specific segment of character pathology and the therapeutic and theoretical flaws in distinguishing needs and wishes, Shabad sets up straw men to make his argument. He uses self psychology and drive theory as foils as he offers his version of a psychoanalytic psychotherapy. The term “self psychology” as such does not appear in the paper, but its code words do, for example, a reference to Mitchell's (1988) metaphors of “damage,” “narcissistic ‘needs,’” therapists who are perceived “to have little or no autonomous existence as distinct from that of being a fantasied selfobject responding, as if on a yo-yo attached directly to the patient's needs,” “the therapist … designated to play the role of selfobject in the patient's private narcissistic drama,” and the use of “one's lovemaking partner as an erotic selfobject vehicle to give pleasure to oneself.”

In Shabad's version of self psychology, a “one-sidedness is most obvious when the therapist is designated to play the role of selfobject in the patient's private narcissistic drama.” He illustrates his view of this one-sidedness when therapists “take on the patient's projection of responsibility and meet the patient's entitled passivity with their own indebted activity.” Shabad is clever at spelling out the blind spots and countertransference dangers he assumes to be operative in the treatment approaches of his foils but does not address the countertransferential effect of his suggestions.

Frank M. Lachmann, Ph.D. is a Senior Supervisor and Training Analyst, Postgraduate Center for Mental Health, New York City, and a member of the Core Faculty, Institute for the Psychoanalytic Study of Subjectivity, New York City.

responseSet.title: Reply to Dimen
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Dimen

Michael Bader DMH

Michael Bader

457 Spruce Street
San Francisco, CA 94118

Michael Bader  

In my essay “Adaptive Sadomasochism and Psychological Growth,” I attempted to show, through two detailed case presentations, how some playful forms of sadomasochistic sexual fantasy and ritual can function as progressive psychological moves in a heterosexual relationship. I felt that this was worth exploring, given the bias within psychoanalytic theory to regard sadomasochistic fantasy as mainly a pathological compromise formation. Second, I felt that, with the exception of Jessica Benjamin, feminist theory had not adequately understood, from a psychoanalytic viewpoint, the meaning of those relatively healthy instances of sadomasochistic sexual fantasy and play in the lives of some heterosexual men and women. My interest was in exploring this phenomenon of “healthy” sadomasochism at a level that was clinically rich and stayed close to the actual subjective experience of the parties involved, since this kind of detailed narrative and clinical analysis is generally missing from a literature that can easily become polemical, partisan, and politically heated.

Muriel Dimen wishes I had written a different paper, one that dealt with issues that are more interesting to her. She is, for instance, extremely interested in language and in the deconstruction of the meaning of my (clearly) vernacular use of such terms as “consensual,” “intuitively,” and “truly weak.” As Dimen repeatedly indicates, while the vernacular meaning is clear to her and the general reader (e.g., by “consensual” I mean “without force”), she is more interested in such issues as whether true consent is possible for heterosexual women within patriarchy, the possible preoedipal roots of intuition, or the relationship of my ideas to Winnicott's notions of the true and false self. I share these interests with Dimen and hope at some future time to contribute to a deeper understanding of them.

responseSet.title: Introduction: Hermeneutics and You
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction

Hermeneutics and You

Steven Cooper PHD

Steven Cooper

875 Massachusetts Avenue, #54
Cambridge, MA 02139

Steven Cooper  

Some psychoanalysts, skeptical or puzzled by the hermeneutic turn in psychoanalytic theory, may at times be reminded of Goethe's frequently quoted aphorism: “Grey, dear friend, is all theory, and green, life's golden tree” (Faust). Indeed, psychoanalysts with a more pragmatic bent may find themselves looking for the clinical kernel within the hermeneutic debate, life's golden tree as it were, or at least trying to find the forest through the trees.

Questions and implications, however, raised by this two-part symposium examining what and how the analyst knows point to the ways in which many clinical and epistemological questions are inextricably linked: what is the heart of the matter and what is the truth of the matter need to be considered simultaneously.

These questions attempt to move the analyst clinically from the high ground of being an expert decipherer to some common ground of being a participant-observer. Despite the enormous variability even within those analysts who maintain a relativist view of truth within the analytic situation, there is generally more and more agreement that the truth of the matter is contextually, not objectively, defined. This view of truth is emphasized well in the following joke about truth and cultural relativism. A fundamentalist Christian pastor was teaching some children a Sunday school lesson and posed a riddle: “What is it that collects nuts for winter, climbs trees, and has a bushy tail?” An eager child was called on by the pastor and said, “Well, I know the answer's Jesus Christ, but it sure sounds like a squirrel to me.”

Dr. Cooper is on the faculty of the Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute and the Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis. He is also Assistant Professor of Psychology, Beth Israel Hospital, Harvard Medical School.

© 1993 The Analytic Press

[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Blechner, Lesser, and Schwartz

Robert Stolorow PHD and Jeffrey Trop MD

Jeffrey Trop

11726 San Vicente Boulevard, Suite 410
Los Angeles, CA 90049

11980 San Vicente Boulevard, Suite 819
Los Angeles, CA 90049

Jeffrey Trop  

The commentaries by Blechner, Lesser, and Schwartz have several features in common. While caricaturing us as covert sexual ideologues, these commentators all use our clinical presentation as a platform for the vehement expression of their own sexual ideologies. They all seem to feel that it is now politically incorrect to publish a case in which the patient, during analysis, chose a heterosexual path over a homosexual (or, more accurately, a largely asexual) one. They all make statements about our patient and his analysis that are starkly at variance with the patient's own inner experience, both as reported by him and as inferred from many years of assiduous empathic inquiry into the nature of his affective experiences with men, with women, and in the transference. They all presume to diagnose aspects of our own subjective worlds—our belief systems (Lesser) and our conscious and unconscious phobias (Blechner) and “philias” (Schwartz)—without the benefit of knowing anything about our personal lives and histories and without ever having engaged in an empathic dialogue with either of us.

All three commentators seem to hold, as an ideal goal for every analysis, the fullest exploration of all possibilities for sexual expression, irrespective of whether the patient wishes to do this or not.

Dr. Stolorow is a Faculty Member and Training and Supervising Analyst, Institute of Contemporary Psychoanalysis, Los Angeles, and a Core Faculty Member, Institute for the Psychoanalytic Study of Subjectivity, New York City.

Dr. Trop is a Faculty Member and Training and Supervising Analyst, Institute of Contemporary Psychoanalysis, Los Angeles, and a Member of the Los Angeles Psychoanalytic Institute and Society, Los Angeles.

responseSet.title: Sex, Gender, and the Analyst's Subjectivity
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sex, Gender, and the Analyst's Subjectivity

Joseph Newirth PHD

Joseph Newirth

51 Longview Road
Port Washington, NY 11050

Joseph Newirth  

This symposium provides a ground-breaking discussion of the effect of the analyst as a gendered subject and object on the evolving processes within the psychoanalytic dyad. These papers represent a significant step beyond our usual notions of transference and countertransference. Ruth Gruenthal and Irwin Hirsch have each presented extraordinarily rich, stimulating, and clinically complex papers in which their presence and their experience of themselves as gendered subjects and objects became an important focus of the analytic dialogue. These are original discussions of the relationship among the patient's experience of the analyst's sexuality and gender, the analyst's experience of his or her own sexuality and gender, and the patient's transferential experience of the analyst as a sexual object. The authors struggle to find new ways to integrate their theoretical positions with their subjective experiences of sex and gender within the developing analytic dialogues. Along with the discussions by Sue Shapiro and Alan Kindler, these papers present a fascinating convergence of thought and technique from three separate schools of psychoanalysis: self psychology, interpersonal psychoanalysis, and object relations theory.

Kindler ironically longs for a past when life and psychoanalysis seemed to be much simpler, when we had the certainty of a one-person psychology in which we knew who the patient was and what to attend to in the clinical process. These papers represent a growing awareness of the complexity of the psychoanalytic process in which the concepts of

Joseph Newirth is the Director of Clinical Training and an Associate Professor in Clinical Psychology at the Derner Institute of Advanced Psychological Studies at Adelphi University, Garden City, New York. He is also on the faculty and is a Supervising and Training Analyst in the Postdoctoral Program in Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy at Adelphi University and at the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Paradox and the Repetitive Search for the Real: Reply to Ghent, Lachmann, and Russell

Peter Shabad PHD

Peter Shabad

6931 N. Kilbourn
Lincolnwood, IL 60646

Peter Shabad  

I would like to thank Drs. Ghent, Lachmann, and Russell for presenting their unique perspectives in their discussions of my paper. Russell presents a lucid and provocative exploration of trauma and the repetition compulsion. My considerations of the symptomatic aftermath of trauma and the repetition compulsion will lead into a broad-based examination of the relevant treatment issues discussed by Ghent and Lachmann.

I agree with Russell's emphasis on the solitariness of trauma. If trauma can be known only “after the fact” as Russell proposes, through its posttraumatic communications, it suggests that the aloneness of not being able to communicate one's experience immediately may be intrinsic to trauma. Russell's near accident into a tree occurred when he was alone. Only 20 minutes later, in the presence of a friend, did Russell display his symptomatology of “feeling faint, dizzy, and sweaty.” My son was more fortunate in that, at the time of the “trauma” of his knee injury, there was someone he could turn to for solace and recognition of what had happened to him; perhaps more significantly, someone else who could bear witness to the “reality” of his experience. As Russell suggests, one may need this sense of connectedness in order to have the freedom to feel.

When no one is there to offer comfort, one may turn inward and be less inclined to acknowledge that what happened “out there” actually happened. One immediately “adapts” to the absence of another by stoically learning how to go it alone and rely on one's own resources, a self-reliance that all too often seems to be one of the residual casualties of growing up.

© 1993 The Analytic Press

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Intersubjective Approach: Commentary on Michael Tansey's “Psychoanalytic Expertise” (PD, 2[3], 1992)

Robert Stolorow PHD

Robert Stolorow

Robert Stolorow  

In a recent article, Tansey (1992) erroneously depicts the intersubjective approach to psychoanalysis as a simple developmental-arrest model that neglects the role of inner conflict and denies the value of interpretation. On the basis of one unusual clinical vignette, he makes the absurd generalization that my collaborators and I (Stolorow, Brandchaft, and Atwood, 1987) “frown upon” an analyst's ever disagreeing with a patient—a prohibition that would be tantamount to requiring that the analyst's subjectivity be banished from the therapeutic system, as if an analyst could gaze upon a patient's experience with pure and presuppositionless eyes.

Contrary to Tansey's mischaracterization, my collaborators and I (Stolorow, 1985; Stolorow and Brandchaft, 1987; Stolorow et al., 1987, chap. 6) have for years been challenging the false dichotomy, perpetuated both by classical analysts and some self psychologists (and now Tansey), between inner conflict and developmental failure, and we have proposed a conceptualization of conflict formation that is consistent with relational-model theorizing. Consider, for example, the following passages:

When conflict is freed from the encumbering image of an energy disposal apparatus and is pictured solely as a subjective state of the person, then the supposed antithesis between conflict theory and self psychology vanishes. When conflict is liberated from the doctrine of the primacy of instinctual drive, then the specific meaning-contexts that give rise to subjective states of conflict becomes an empirical question to be explored psychoanalytically. The focus of psychoanalytic inquiry then shifts from the presumed vicissitudes of drive to the intersubjective contexts in which conflict states crystallize … [Stolorow et al., 1987, p. 89].

© 1993 The Analytic Press

Ego Psychological and Object Relational Approaches — Is it Either/Or?: Commentary on Neil Altman's “Psychoanalysis and the Urban Poor”

Anni Bergman PHD

Anni Bergman

224 West 20th Street
New York, NY 10011

Anni Bergman  

In a recent issue of Psychoanalytic Dialogues, Lewis Aron (1992) states, “Much of the tradition of clinical psychiatry and psychoanalysis has been that the clinician sees himself or herself as healthy and mature and looks down subtly or blatantly at the patient as sick and immature” (pp. 182-183). Later in the same paper he refers to Ferenczi, who believed that analysts tended to blame their failures on their patients by pointing to their resistances or to their unanalyzability. Aron says:

Ferenczi was a devoted healer who placed primary emphasis on helping patients and was less concerned with building a theoretical system…. He believed that an analyst should try to help any patient willing to come for analysis and that failure points not to the patient's resistance exclusively but to our own inadequacies and personal and theoretical limitations [p. 183].

When we treat patients from impoverished backgrounds, we must face not only our own personal and theoretical limitations, but also the limitations of a health system that does not give us the necessary freedom and time to work effectively. This dilemma is very well illustrated in “Psychoanalysis and the Urban Poor,” where Neil Altman shows in some detail how the health system works against the therapeutic process. For example, when Medicaid does not pay for missed sessions and thereby

Elliot Adler

117 East 37th Street
New York, NY 10016

Elliot Adler  

In thinking over Dr. Franks' thoughtful and stimulating paper, I found myself recalling an exchange that occurred during the discussion period at a recent professional panel in which I participated. A number of us, representing a diverse range of theoretical orientations on the psychoanalytic spectrum, had been asked to present our views of some extensive case material. We had all gone on in considerable length about the psychodynamics, the transference, and the countertransference in the more or less predictable ways we have all come to expect in these situations, when a member of the audience got up and asked, “But what do you want him [i.e., the patient] to do?” We were all a little nonplussed by this question, which was asked in an insistent tone of voice that indicated that the questioner believed he was raising a perfectly reasonable issue that we had unaccountably been ignoring.

What do we want him to do? I puzzled to myself. What possible context of meaning, frame of reference, or point of connection with anything we were talking about could he be referring to! All could make of it was that the questioner was somehow an outsider who probably did not get what we were about. Fortunately, the senior discussant on the panel, an erudite and very seasoned analyst, was quicker on the uptake than the rest of us. He alone recognized that this gentleman was asking a question about our theory of cure: what did we want the patient to do in

responseSet.title: Reply to Gill, Jacobs, and Shapiro
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Gill, Jacobs, and Shapiro

Morris Eagle PHD

Morris Eagle

Morris Eagle  

I am grateful that Drs. Gill, Jacobs, and Shapiro have taken the time and trouble to comment on my paper. I found their remarks interesting and have learned something from all of them.

Dr. Shapiro

What I found especially interesting about Shapiro's paper is what can be seen as his metatheoretical perspective on the therapeutic function of theories. Shapiro takes the position that just about any psychoanalytic theory of treatment may equally serve important therapeutic functions such as providing the therapist with a safe haven when he or she experiences confusion and distressing affects. I am inclined generally to agree with Shapiro, although I would like to believe that there are limits to his proposition. That is, it is possible that there are some theories that are so generally off the mark or so inappropriately imposed on, and specifically off the mark with, a given patient that their theoretical content does make a therapeutic difference. Thus, for any given patient it may not be the case that all theories are equal with regard to “fittingness” and therapeutic effectiveness. Ultimately, this is an empirical issue (i.e., whether, to what degree, and in what ways the theoretical persuasion of the therapist makes a difference in therapeutic outcome) that, although testable in principle, would be extremely difficult to investigate in a rigorous way.

I think Shapiro is correct in alerting us to both the usefulness and dangers connected with adopting a particular theoretical position in clinical work. It is inevitable and perhaps even desirable that one work from a particular theoretical position. And yet, there is always the danger of imposing one's theoretical bias on the patient's reports. I suspect that we have all experienced the operation of such biases, observed it, or both.

[[RunningHead]]

Centers of Moral Gravity: Commentary on Donald Spence's “The Hermeneutic Turn”

Richard Rorty PHD

Richard Rorty

412 Cabell Hall
University of Virginia
Charlottesville, VA 22903

Richard Rorty  

The approach to psychoanalysis that Donald Spence takes in his paper is roughly the same as the one he took in his Narrative Truth and Historical Truth: Meaning and Interpretation in Psycho-analysis (Spence, 1982). That approach seems to me just right. I think that psychoanalysis has suffered long enough from the aftereffects of Freud's awkward attempts to force it into the mold of “positive science.” I agree with Spence that Freud's attempt to model psychoanalysis on archaeology founders on the fact that, as Freud (1937) said about the Wolf Man case, “an assured conviction of the truth of the constructions … achieves the same therapeutic result as a recaptured memory” (p. 226). Since my own pragmatist philosophical views lead me to treat the distinction between finding and making as a difference that often makes no difference, I do not see much point in questions about whether the analyst discovers or invents the patient's unconscious. This question, like the dispute among philosophers of science about whether neutrinos are “objectively real” or just immensely convenient heuristic fictions, strikes me as an uninteresting verbal dispute. But, although my pragmatism leads me to agree with the strategy of Spence's paper, it also leads me to suggest, as Jerome Bruner does, that Spence push his conclusions a bit further.

I think that what Spence says in his paper is reinforced by a recent view of the self and of the nature of psychological inquiry—a view is, so to speak, tailor-made for us pragmatists.

Richard Rorty has taught philosophy at Wellesley and Princeton and is currently University Professor of the Humanities at the University of Virginia.

An earlier version of this article was presented at a meeting of the Division of Psychoanalysis (Division 39) of the American Psychological Association, April 1992.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Wish, Need, and Neediness: Commentary on Shabad's “Resentment, Indignation, Entitlement”

Emmanuel Ghent MD

Emmanuel Ghent

131 Prince Street
New York, NY 10012

Emmanuel Ghent  

Dr. Shabad presents a perspicacious and moving account of how childhood wishes that were forced underground as a result of the traumatic reception they received at the hands of parenting adults later emerge from Hades as needs bearing the qualities of importunity, peremptoriness, and entitlement, often blended with veiled or not so veiled resentment, anger, fury, and vengefulness. He proposes an approach to the therapeutic handling of people with this type of problem, the crux of which is the acceptance and revitalization of the unconscious wishes by interpretive means. He urges caution in the application of any approach, such as self psychology, that hints of gratifying responsiveness to patients' “needs.” Shabad's paper, for me, engages the question of what is therapeutic about therapy or analysis and extends to an even more general question: how do people change and grow emotionally, whether in or out of analysis?

Much of the paper is given to a remarkably detailed and often sympathetic account of the plight, both inner and outer, of a person caught in a tangle of malignant need, a phrase I extrapolate from Balint's (1968) description of “malignant regression.” Shabad is at pains in this paper to show how it is possible to avert, in the therapeutic situation, the intransigence of such a state of need by encouraging the patient to “take responsibility for,” or reclaim ownership of, the exiled wishes.

Emmanuel Ghent is on the faculty of the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, where he is chairman of the Relational Track and Clinical Professor of Psychology.

[[RunningHead]]

The Social Politics of Psychoanalysis Commentary on Neil Altman's: “Psychoanalysis and the Urban Poor”**

RoseMarie Foster PHD

RoseMarie Foster

155 East 29 St., #5D
New York, NY 10016

RoseMarie Foster  

In his 1918 address to the Fifth International Psychoanalytic Congress, Freud offered his clearest assertion of the social obligation of psychoanalysis (Jacoby, 1983). He acknowledged that large masses of people suffer from neurosis as much as the smaller numbers of rich. Freud openly admitted, however, that the financial necessities of the analyst's existence “limit our work to the well-to-do classes” (Freud, 1918). Nevertheless, “the poor man has as much right to assistance for his mind.” Freud proposed an idea for the future when “the conscience of the community will awake” and provide free psychoanalytic care for the wider social strata (Freud, 1918).

As the history of the psychoanalytic movement has shown us, however, Freud's vision was split in two. On one hand, there were followers who took this social consciousness quite seriously. In 1923 Ernst Simmel and Max Eitinger organized the Berlin Institute, composed of a circle of early analysts with liberal social and political ideals. From its inception this institute was committed to providing therapy to those who lacked financial means. Motivated by its own social ideology, this early circle was committed to emancipating psychoanalysis from its exclusive reliance on wealthy neurotics (Lewy, 1947; Peck, 1966; Jacoby, 1983).

Subsequently, in the wake of the Nazi era, when the psychoanalytic movement was forcibly dispersed to other parts of the world, the vision of a social consciousness was kept alive by some factions.

Dr. Foster is an Adjunct Associate Professor of Psychology, City College, The City University of New York, and a Senior Supervisor in the Bilingual Treatment Program, Bellevue Hospital Center.

*This paper is dedicated to the memory of R., A., and C., who were the sources of my own social politics.

Enactments, Transference, and Symptomatic Cure: A Case History

Morris Eagle PHD

Morris Eagle

252 Bloor St. W.
Toronto, Ontario
M5S IV6

Morris Eagle  

I present a case study in which the dramatic disappearance of the patient's chronic symptom of dyspareunia followed a critical episode in the treatment in which a core dynamic theme of defiance-punishment was enacted in the patient-therapist interaction. Symptomatic cure was not accompanied by the patient's explicit insight, but rather seemed to be a function of a complex process of test-passing and transference-countertransference enactments leading to the disconfirmation of a core unconscious pathogenic belief. Concepts of transference, transference cure, misalliance cure, and analysis of transference are discussed in the context of this case study.

[[RunningHead]]

Interaction and Interpretation: Commentary on Morris Eagle's “Enactments, Transference, and Symptomatic Cure”

Merton Gill MD

Merton Gill

P.O. Box 6998
Chicago, IL 60680

Merton Gill  

I am glad to have the opportunity to discuss Morris Eagle's thoughtful and interesting report of a therapy and his discussion of a number of important points in the theory of the therapeutic process, applicable to psychoanalysis as well as to so-called psychoanalytic psychotherapy.

Interpretation and Interaction

I am, of course, pleased that he ascribes to me a role in changing his view from a one-person to a two-person perspective on the therapeutic process, which is one way I would characterize his shift to seeing the therapeutic process as a “transference-countertransference dance.” I will later argue that both one-person and two-person perspectives are necessary to understand the therapeutic situation, but I first deal with only the two-person perspective. To speak of this perspective as one of transference, countertransference, or both can be misleading because these terms are inevitably understood in the usual sense of “distortion” on the patient's part and failure to remain “neutral” on the therapist's part when, in fact, what I mean by the two-person perspective is that the entire therapy situation is one of patient-therapist interaction in which the two people are coparticipants, each acting upon and being acted upon by the other.

Dr. Gill is Professor Emeritus of Psychiatry, University of Illinois Medical Center at Chicago, and Faculty and Supervising Analyst, Institute for Psychoanalysis, Chicago.

[[RunningHead]]

Relational Concepts in Psychoanalysis: An Integration by Stephen A. Mitchell

(Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1988, ix + 306 pp., $25.00)

Janet Bachant PHD and Arnold Richards MD

Arnold Richards

205 West 86th Street
New York, NY 10024

200 East 89th Street, #45C
New York, NY 10128

Arnold Richards  

In relational concepts in psychoanalysis: an integration, Stephen A. Mitchell presents a challenging, creative articulation of the ways in which relations with others shape and color psychic life. Bringing prodigious talent and breadth of knowledge to his task, Mitchell portrays contemporary psychoanalysis as dominated by two competing perspectives: Freud's drive theory and a cluster of theories that he depicts as relationally based. Mitchell sets as his goal the unification of an array of relational perspectives (primarily British object relations theory, interpersonal psychoanalysis, and self psychology) into a comprehensive theoretical framework. This perspective, which describes relations with others as the building blocks of psychic structure, is contrasted with aspects of Freudian drive theory throughout the book and occasionally with contemporary classical thought.

Within Mitchell's framework, “mind is composed of relational configurations” (p. 3). Mind becomes dyadic in nature and is defined as “transactional patterns and internal structures derived from an interactive, interpersonal field” (p. 17).

Janet Lee Bachant is a Senior Supervisor and on the faculty of the Adult Training Program and the Supervisory Training Program at the Postgraduate Center for Mental Health. She is also a member of the teaching faculty at the Westchester Institute for Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy.

Dr. Richards is a Training and Supervising Analyst at the New York Psychoanalytic Institute and the Editor of the American Psychoanalyst, the quarterly newsletter of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

The authors wish to thank Susan Knapp for reading and providing helpful commentary on an earlier draft of this paper.

responseSet.title: Countertransference Enactments and Some Issues Related to External Factors in the Analyst's Life
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Countertransference Enactments and Some Issues Related to External Factors in the Analyst's Life

Irwin Hirsch PHD

Irwin Hirsch

1311 Lexington Avenue, Ste. 1A
New York, NY 10128

Irwin Hirsch  

Beyond the inevitability of countertransference feelings is the question of countertransference enactments. From a two-person, participant-observer or observing-participant perspective, enactments are inevitable. The analyst becomes influenced by the patient (and influences the patient as well) and enmeshed in the patient's internalized interpersonal configurations. Analysis works not by avoiding such action but by analyzing from within the interactional system. Analysts who are different from one another become engaged in different ways, since the person of the analyst is a significant variable. This article, using case examples, explores two analyst-related variables, age and family configuration, to expand the examination of countertransference enactments and some effects on the analytic process.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Gender-Role Stereotypes and Clinical Process: Commentary on Papers by Gruenthal and Hirsch

Sue Shapiro PHD

Sue Shapiro

14 East 4th Street
New York, NY 10012

Sue Shapiro  

These two authors address gender quite differently. For Hirsch gender is something of a background variable since he is explicitly focusing on the impact of the analyst's age and family structure. As I will show, however, gender is as central in his work as it is in Gruenthal's. In addition, the linkage of these two clinical papers by relational theorists, one of whom is a self psychologist and the other an interpersonalist, offers an opportunity for further explication of the differences between these two approaches (see Mitchell, 1992). While both Gruenthal and Hirsch see themselves as using a two-person psychology, what they mean by this seems quite different, and this difference has clear implications for their view of their own participation in the treatments they conduct. While countertransference is the focus of Hirsch's discussion, it remains very much in the background of Gruenthal's presentation.

The juxtaposition of these two articles underscores the enormous complexity of comparative psychoanalysis and the difficulty in teasing apart any given variable for discussion. The style and data of these two articles, undoubtedly like the people who wrote them and the treatments they conduct, differ considerably. One has only to ask oneself what it would be like to be in treatment with either of these authors to realize that besides their obvious differences in gender and theoretical orientation, we are clearly in the presence of two very different personalities.

Dr. Shapiro is a Supervising Analyst at the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. She is also Director of the Center for the Study and Treatment of Abuse and Incest at the Manhattan Institute for Psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: Loyal Opposition and the Clarity of Dissent: Commentary on Donald P. Spence's “The Hermeneutic Turn”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Loyal Opposition and the Clarity of Dissent: Commentary on Donald P. Spence's “The Hermeneutic Turn”

Jerome Bruner PHD

Jerome Bruner

249 Sullivan St.
New York, NY 10012

Jerome Bruner  

I have no complaints about Don Spence's hermeneuticism. My sole complaint, rather, is that he gets homesick for the old positivism he has left behind even before it disappears over the horizon. But I have great sympathy for that kind of agoraphobia, for I know all too well how lonely it can get once one leaves the ancestral mansion of positivist science with its familiar furnishings — its scientific explanations, its objectivity, its error-proof methods, and the ornate, if lapsed, ritual letter of credit that goes with it all. To put it bluntly, I do not like the stance of “loyal opposition” that he proposes: indeed, I find it a self-defeating stance in general, particularly so in a debate that seems to me to be more a prelude to revolution than an attempt to write a compromise budget. For these are revolutionary times in the human sciences, part of a revolution, I think, that extends well beyond the decorous limits of learned debate. And besides, to make the matter more local, compromises seem rarely to work well in the psychoanalytic movement where dissent has traditionally been treated as a form of heresy.

But I suspect that there are good psychological (or, at least, dramaturgic) reasons why we are all tempted to characterize our dissent from positivism as “loyal opposition.” I suppose that even our scientific identities are composed of competing selves.

Dr. Bruner is Research Professor of Psychology and Senior Research Fellow in Law, New York University; formerly, Professor of Psychology, Harvard University, and Watts Professor of Psychology, University of Oxford.

An earlier version of this article was presented at a meeting of the Division of Psychoanalysis (Division 39) of the American Psychological Association, April 1992.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Heterophilia — The Love That Dare Not Speak Its Aim: Commentary on Trop and Stolorow's “Defense Analysis in Self Psychology: A Developmental View”**

David Schwartz PHD

David Schwartz

1 786 Pinesbridge Road
Ossining, NY 10562

David Schwartz  

Trop and stolorow have presented case in which same-sex desire is prominent and treated as pathological. While this pathologizing view of same-sex desire in their paper can be readily illustrated, what seems more important is their apparent decision to maintain their point of view without actually discussing it. In this way, their paper exemplifies two interrelated phenomena seen in psychoanalysis: (1) the unarticulated belief in a particular sexual ideology, which I call “heterophilia,” and (2) the explicit belief that psychoanalysis is, or should be, free of ideology and politics.

In what follows I try to show how heterophilia, the overvaluing of intimate relations between different-sexed partners, manifests itself in Trop and Stolorow's case report and how it tends to immunize their ideological commitments against articulation and scrutiny. Moreover, I will try to show that as an artifact of heterophilia, same-sex desire is necessarily denigrated. These points are in line with the general claim of this paper that the particular sexual ideology of heterophilia and its discursive practices detour psychoanalytic work from its expansive and liberatory aims.

*Psychoanalytic Dialogues, 2(4):427-442, 1992.David Schwartz is a psychoanalyst in private practice in Manhattan and Westchester. He is a member of the Psychoanalytic Society of the Westchester Center for the Study of Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy.

© 1993 The Analytic Press

responseSet.title: The Hermeneutic Turn: Soft Science or Loyal Opposition?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Hermeneutic Turn: Soft Science or Loyal Opposition?

Donald Spence PHD

Donald Spence

Box 101
Rutgers Medical School
University Hts.
Piscataway, NJ 08854

Donald Spence  

The hermeneutic turn in psychoanalysis may be less a move toward soft science than an attempt to address certain failures in the classical tradition. The latter has failed to convert Freud's many metaphors into anything like responsible science; it has been unable to give up its fascination with Freud and move on to other thinkers and other points of view; it has been unable to find an answer to the problem of secrecy and privileged access to clinical data; under the heavy hand of theory, it has all but eliminated research from the psychoanalytic enterprise; and finally, it has been unable to find a home for the Roshomon effect and the fact that almost everything worth looking at has at least two sides. Above all, psychoanalysis needs to recognize that our theory is often the projection of either our Zeitgeist or our personal history. Until we can make room for the problem of subjectivity in our theory-building, we will never be able to separate the singer from the song.

[[RunningHead]]

Insight and Experience: Commentary on Morris Eagle's “Enactments, Transference, and Symptomatic Cure”

Theodore Jacobs MD

Theodore Jacobs

170 East 77th Street, #1G
New York, NY 10021

Theodore Jacobs  

In his intriguing contribution, “Enactments, Transference, and Symptomatic Cure,” Dr. Eagle has written the kind of paper rarely attempted by the author of a case study. In the light of the recently published views of Gill, Weiss and Sampson, and others concerning the importance of here-and-now interactions between patient and therapist, he has reexamined his material, concluded that he overlooked certain critical transitions in his work with Ms. S, and developed some intriguing ideas about the role that these experiences played in the therapeutic process.

The critical reexamination by a therapist of a previously treated case is an unusual occurrence in our field. It is, however, a uniquely valuable way for therapists to assess their work and to gain a fresh perspective on the changes that have taken place in their thinking and clinical approach over time. Eagle is to be congratulated for undertaking the reexamination of a fascinating case and for sharing the results of his reflections with us. In doing so he has provided us with an opportunity not only to assess the factors that led to a remarkable change in his patient but to review some of the ideas on which his reevaluation of the treatment is based.

Briefly summarized, Eagle's understanding of the key transactions that took place between Ms. S and him is as follows. During one session Ms. S toyed with the top two buttons on her blouse, buttoning and unbuttoning them in an unconsciously seductive manner. This behavior had a stimulating effect on Eagle, and, by way of response, he suggested in a subsequent session that Ms. S consider using the couch.

Dr. Jacobs is Clinical Professor of Psychiatry at Albert Einstein College of Medicine. He is also Training and Supervising Analyst at New York Psychoanalytic Institute and at Psychoanalytic Institute, New York University.

© 1993 The Analytic Press

responseSet.title: Theory and Change: Commentary on Morris Eagle's “Enactments, Transference, and Symptomatic Cure”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Theory and Change: Commentary on Morris Eagle's “Enactments, Transference, and Symptomatic Cure”

Robert Shapiro PHD

Robert Shapiro

115 East 87th Street, 32A
New York, NY 10128

Robert Shapiro  

Dr. eagle has clearly demonstrated the way a skilled psychoanalyst with a solid theoretical orientation can bring new understanding to a clinical presentation. What is rare is the analyst's doing this in relation to his or her own case material. The two analyses of S, unlike its prototype, Kohut's “The two analyses of Mr. Z,” reanalyze the data rather than the patient. In a sense Eagle served as the discussant of his own paper. He adds a relational perspective to his original orthodox view. He then attempts to fit the outcome into this new model.

As the discussant, he has followed the tradition of our field and highlighted what has been overlooked by the “naive” presenter. By being such an astute discussant, Eagle has focused on an array of important issues about both the patient and the nature of psychoanalytic cure. In his clinical presentation, Eagle has elucidated the intricate and often inexplicable combination of variables that contribute to a patient's achieving greater satisfaction in living.

Eagle has empathized, offered advice, interpreted, provided a “corrective emotional experience,” created a benign and caring environment, participated in the enactment of an array of internal scenarios, and offered an intellectual structure by which S can understand her past and present experience.

We are confronted with many more questions than answers. Given the complexity of what has transpired, what facets of the treatment were central in bringing about the alleviation of symptoms? Is this a real cure or a transference cure? Does it matter? Have the positive therapeutic results been brought about by Eagle's neutrality or by his seductiveness?

Dr. Shapiro is a Fellow and a Training and Supervising Analyst at the William Alanson White Institute.

© 1993 The Analytic Press

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Active Intervention, Psychic Structure, and the Analysis of Transference: Commentary on Frank's “Action, Insight, and Working Through”

Paul Wachtel PHD

Paul Wachtel

79 West 12th Street
New York, NY 10011

Paul Wachtel  

Freud, it is well known, viewed psychoanalysis as not one thing but three—a theory of human behavior and experience, a method of therapy based on that theory, and an avenue for research. The issues raised by considering the relation between traditional psychoanalytic methods and those developed by therapists operating from other orientations bear not just on therapeutic practice but on all three dimensions. The implications of the clinical perspective that Frank articulates so helpfully are perhaps ultimately even more significant for psychoanalytic theory than for how we approach daily practice, for they bear on the very nature of psychic structures and how they should be conceived. The stakes are thus very high, and the matters Frank addresses are in fact not exotic frills at the fringes of psychoanalytic concern but matters that potentially reach to the heart of the psychoanalytic enterprise.

For this reason I begin my discussion of Frank's excellent and stimulating contribution by addressing a point that he makes almost incidentally but that I believe bears rather centrally on the issues his paper is addressing.

Paul L. Wachtel, Ph.D. is CUNY Distinguished Professor at The City University of New York, where he teaches in the doctoral program in clinical psychology. He is also on the faculty of the Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis at New York University.

© 1993 The Analytic Press

responseSet.title: Reply to Bachant and Richards
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Bachant and Richards

Stephen Mitchell PHD

Stephen Mitchell

251 West 71st Street, #1B
New York, NY 10023

Stephen Mitchell  

The prospect of responding to this extensive critique of my book is quite daunting. Bachant and Richards find so many problems on so many different levels, I thought at first that it would take another book to clarify my positions or to correct them. (Most important, I had to decide how serious a flaw it was to be a “dichotomizer,” which sounds to me like the title of a sinister movie starring Arnold Schwarzenegger.) As I reflected further on their arguments, however, I began to realize that underlying all the issues they raise is a problem in the way they read or, I should say, misread my work. Their kind of misreading is quite representative of the response of many analysts who think in traditional categories about current developments in analytic theorizing and practice. So it seems important to try to understand the difficulties they have in their encounter with postclassical theorizing like mine.

My discussion has two parts. First, I sketch out the methodology of their criticism and give some examples of the way in which it leads them to miss or misunderstand my ideas. Second, I discuss the more general and fundamental political issue that separates their position from mine and the implications of those positions for our very different readings of the history of psychoanalytic ideas and the current state of classical Freudian thought.

Relational Concepts in Psychoanalysis was never intended, in itself, as a comprehensive theory of anything, much less as a complete alternative to classical Freudian theory. As I stated explicitly, I was offering an approach, suggesting ways in which different relational traditions might be integrated, pointing the direction in which things might profitably move. Bachant and Richards pick specific issues that I in no way claimed to be

Dr. Mitchell, in addition to being editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues, is a Training and Supervising Analyst at William Alanson White Institute. He is a Supervisor and a member of the faculty, New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis and the Urban Poor
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and the Urban Poor**

Neil Altman PHD

Neil Altman

425 Riverside Drive #13D
New York, NY 10025

Neil Altman  

This paper considers, from a psychoanalytic point of view, work with urban poor patients. A previous attempt, from an ego psychological perspective, to adapt psychoanalytic theory and technique for work with such patients is reviewed. An attempt is then made to elaborate an object relations perspective, based largely on the work of Fairbairn. The two approaches are compared and contrasted, especially with respect to common problems that arise in work with this group of people, such as missed appointments, crises, and requests for “concrete” help. Finally, there is a review of how the structure and policies of the public clinics in which most poor patients are seen impact on analytically oriented work.

responseSet.title: Illusions of Candor: Reply to Sass
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Illusions of Candor: Reply to Sass

Charles Spezzano PHD

Charles Spezzano

2202 Fillmore Street
San Francisco, CA 94115

Charles Spezzano  

While working on this response to Louis Sass's scathing critique of my essay on a psychoanalytic epistemology, I came across a quote so close in spirit and content to one of my key arguments that I could not resist using it to open my remarks here. The author, describing a particular epistemological position he found useful and compelling, wrote that “although it does not proffer the Cartesian dream of absolute certainty,” it does “hold out the promise of a valid and public kind of knowledge—one based not on some kind of timeless certainty but on the relative persuasiveness, within a particular interpretive community, of the various interpretations offered.” The author has the same target in sight as I did—the Cartesian fantasy of absolute certainty. He urges us to give up the hope for “timeless certainty,” a sentiment obviously identical in spirit to my argument against the quest for certainty. Finally, he argues for the viability of equating the truth-value of an argument with its persuasiveness within the professional conversations taking place within a particular established discipline.

The striking similarities between this author's position and mine might lead one to assume that Sass would find him to be as much of a misguided relativist as he accused me of being. That is unlikely, however, because the author is Sass (1988, p. 254). So why, I was forced to ask, is he saying those terrible things about me for taking a position he apparently also espouses? There are two clues to this mystery in his essay: he focuses on my use of the word “conversation,” and he spends a great deal of time discussing a criticism I made of a criticism he made of Donald Spence in 1988. Somehow, Sass equates my use of the word “conversation” with Richard Geha's use of the word “fiction.” He writes as if he read me to mean that by calling psychoanalysis a conversation, it has nothing to do with the quest for truth. I made it very clear, however, that I view all scientific and scholarly disciplines as ongoing conversations.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Essential Invisibility of Trauma and the Need for Repetition: Commentary on Shabad's “Resentment, Indignation, Entitlement”

Paul Russell MD

Paul Russell

158 Winthrop Road
Brookline, MA 02146

Paul Russell  

Peter Shabad has singled out for investigation a phenomenon of compelling interest and, I think, does so very usefully. There is a way in which a seriously and cumulatively traumatized person attempts to avoid the pain of recognition of the past. In ways that are extremely difficult to pinpoint, this ongoing failure of recognition is intimately connected with the individual's present-day effectiveness and adaptation are compromised. Shabad focuses on the capacity for genuine emotional reciprocity and the capacity to give and receive as especially vulnerable to the residues of trauma.

My own interests in recent years have been similar, namely, the ways in which trauma is intimately connected with the repetition compulsion. The traumatized person attempts to avoid the pain of recognition of the past and in so doing re-creates (presumably unwittingly) the experience of injury and arranges relationships in such a way that the sort of interpersonal experience that is most potentially helpful becomes in this regard what is least likely to occur. How and why this should be the case is, by all appearances, “uncanny” (Freud, 1919) and “daemonic” (Freud, 1920). It is also obviously of profound interest to anyone with the temerity to offer help. One could make a case for defining our enterprise as the attempt to help in those contexts where a person's relationship with his past makes that help less probable than it would have been otherwise.

Dr. Russell is on the staff of the Beth Israel Hospital and on the faculty of the Boston Psychoanalytic Institute and the Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: Reply to Bergman and Foster
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Bergman and Foster

Neil Altman PHD

Neil Altman

425 Riverside Drive, #13D
New York, NY 10025

Neil Altman  

PERHAPS AS A CONSEQUENCE of the concentration of psychoanalytic practitioners in private practice, we have been slow to build bridges between our rich theoretical and technical discussions and the social and political discussions in which many of us are also engaged, sometimes deeply and passionately. I am grateful to Psychoanalytic Dialogues for sponsoring this integrative effort, which I have found fascinating and stimulating. I am also grateful to RoseMarie Peréz Foster and Anni Bergman for their valuable contributions to this conversation, which I hope will continue and expand.

In responding to the commentaries, I would like to focus on a few of the many important points that they raise. I first address the question that Bergman raises in her title: do we have to choose between ego psychological and object relational approaches? I argue that we do have to choose and that Bergman herself, despite her integrative intention and effort, makes such a choice in her way of thinking about, and working with, the patients she discusses. Next, I consider some of the consequences of the theoretical choices that I believe we inevitably, and more or less explicitly, make. Finally, in addressing Peréz Foster's commentary, I note that she and I found a compatibility between our social and political views and our psychoanalytic theoretical learnings. I elaborate on some of the implications with respect to a very knotty question: on what basis do we establish our theoretical commitments in psychoanalysis?

Greenberg and Mitchell (1983) state that theoretical models in psychoanalysis are comprehensive, that both “drive/structure” and “relational/structure” models can find a place for all mental phenomena within the conceptual network established by each. Thus, ego psychologists can find a place for object relations within their model, while object relationists can find a place in their model for ego functions. This ability to assimilate all phenomena within one's theoretical model can create the illusion that one is transcending theoretical boundaries.

responseSet.title: Reply to Commentaries by Kindler and Shapiro
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Commentaries by Kindler and Shapiro

Irwin Hirsch PHD

Irwin Hirsch

1311 Lexington Avenue
New York, NY 10128

Irwin Hirsch  

I thank Drs. Kindler and Shapiro for their thoughtful and intelligent discussions. Both clearly understand my main thesis: countertransference enactments are both inevitable and related to the unique person of the analyst. Since Shapiro's comments are more extensive and detailed, I limit my reply to her critique.

Gender-based rigidities in outlook and gender-related anxieties are the primary focal point of Shapiro's commentary. Her paper is a substantial contribution to the excellent burgeoning literature in this area, an area that women seem to grasp and articulate far better than men. I agree with most of her observations about my own insufficiently questioned gender assumptions, biases, blindness, and anxieties. I feel embarrassed by some of them for they seem so glaring to me after reading her comments. I agree fully that gender is a culturally constructed category and that staying “in gender” in an unchallenged way is the easiest path for most analysts. I also agree that women have traditionally submitted to starry-eyed, eroticized idealizations of their older male analysts and that male analysts have enjoyed this pleasure immensely, without sufficiently challenging this joint construction or repetition (see Hirsch, 1980-1981, 1992). Indeed, without openness to a wider breadth of gender possibilities, we stand to constrict severely the range of our patients' choices. Gender-based anxieties and assumptions are rampant among analysts, and Shapiro illustrates how they operate out of awareness in my own work, particularly with female patients. Her discussion and the literature she cites are eye-opening for all of us who are somewhat gender-embedded and who may not sufficiently challenge “normal” configurations, partly because of living “normal” lives ourselves. As Shapiro notes, most of my colleagues and I are more comfortable in countertransference positions that are “in gender.” Also, most heterosexual analysts are somewhat, to extremely, ill at ease with homosexual feelings toward our patients.

responseSet.title: Adaptive Sadomasochism and Psychological Growth
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Adaptive Sadomasochism and Psychological Growth

Michael Bader DMH

Michael Bader

457 Spruce Street
San Francisco, CA 94118

Michael Bader  

This paper argues that some forms of sadomasochistic fantasy and enactments can represent a developmental and therapeutic advance for some patients. Playful fantasies of dominance and submission, when enacted, function to reassure the patient that the other can survive the full expression and power of his or her sexual desire. Sadomasochistic scenarios can mitigate guilt and worry and free the patient for an experience of sexual ruthlessness. The apparent differences in the manifest roles of the man and woman in this situation are belied in a paradoxical way by underlying commonalities. A woman can use her consensually scripted domination as a reassurance that the man can withstand the full force of her excitement, and the man can experience his partner's excitement in submission as a condition of safety for the consolidation of his phallic strength. Two clinical examples illustrate these paradoxes and the affirmative dimension of certain kinds of sadomasochistic play. The suggestion is made that blanket critiques of sadomasochism as either perversion or misogyny are misguided and potentially repressive.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on Michael J. Bader's “Adaptive Sadomasochism and Psychological Growth”

Muriel Dimen PHD

Muriel Dimen

3 East 10 Street
Suite 1B1
New York, NY 10003

Muriel Dimen  

Michael bader's “adaptive sadomasochism and psychological growth,” a contribution to current thinking about adult sexuality, also provides the opportunity to examine the place of feminist theory in what amounts to a revision of the classical interpretation of sadomasochism as perversion. Recent writings argue that what was once regarded as perverse is in fact acceptable, necessary, desirable. Stoller (1979), for example, thinks aggression is sexuality's core. Bollas (1989) links ruthlessness to mutual orgasm. Kernberg (1991) argues that sadism and masochism are run-of-the-mill components of adult sexuality. And, in a yet unpublished paper, Aron (1991) construes the internalized primal scene as a source of creativity, not trauma.

Michael Bader's intervention contends that sadomasochistic sexual fantasy and enactment can loosen desire and expand sexual possibility. If they are experienced as achievements, not pathological compulsions, sadomasochistic scenarios can also nourish mastery of developmental dilemmas. One such dilemma, according to Bader, is the guilt many women feel about their sexual desire. Women, he says, tend to experience their sexual potency as damaging to their mothers' narcissism. The ensuing guilt inhibits their ruthlessness and aggression and thereby damps their sexual pleasure and agency. This inhibition may intensify in relation to fathers who, unable “to accept and appreciate” their daughters' sexuality, are “either sexually threatened or overstimulated by it.”

Dr. Dimen is Clinical Professor of Psychology, Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, New York University, and a Fellow at the New York Institute for the Humanities, New York University.

© 1993 The Analytic Press

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Patient's Transference Experience of the Analyst's Gender: Projection, Factuality, Interpretation, or Construction

Ruth Gruenthal MMS

Ruth Gruenthal

300 West End Avenue
New York, NY 10023

Ruth Gruenthal  

In this paper I reverse the more commonly posed question, how does the analyst's gender affect transference? Instead, I examine the implications of the analyst's view of the organization of transference in the analytic situation for understanding gender-salient transference formation. I suggest that the analyst's viewpoint on the organization of transference is focal in his or her conception of gender-linked transference themes. I demonstrate that even in the context of a shared theoretical outlook, variations in a one-person psychology perspective on transference entail contradictory findings. I discuss a reformulation of transference, utilizing the contribution of self psychology. It demonstrates a combined one-person, two-person psychology view applied to the organization of a two-dimensional transference. Gender-specific, object-related, and selfobject facets of the transference are codetermined by the patient's psychology and the current ongoing analyst-patient interaction. I contrast conceptualizations of gender-salient transference from within a continuum of a one-person psychology and from a combined one and two-person psychology view. I propose that a combined one- and two-person psychology view on the organization of a two-dimensional transference guides a more encompassing understanding of patients' transference experience of analysts' gender. In a clinical vignette I illustrate the application and clinical utility of this perspective.

Ruth Gruenthal is on the faculty and is a Supervisor, New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. She is also a Training Analyst, a member of the faculty, and a Supervisor, Postgraduate Center for Mental Health, New York City.

© 1993 The Analytic Press

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Life Used To Be So Easy: Commentary on Papers by Gruenthal and Hirsch

Alan Kindler M.B.B.S., F.R.C.P.(C)

Alan Kindler

Medical Arts Bldg, #433
170 St. George Street
Toronto, Ontario M5R 2M8
Canada

Alan Kindler  

If I had known at the beginning that my role as an analyst would become so complicated, I probably would never have started. Now, not only do my theoretical constructs help to shape my patients' experience of me (Gruenthal), but my stage of life and family formation profoundly influence the way I experience and respond to my patients (Hirsch). My theory and my age are thus part of the problem my patients and I face together. Before long, I fear, I will be told that my very character structure, “the organization of my self,” is the most significant element in the treatment relationship, even more important than my theory and technique. Where will it end? Have I no privacy as an analyst, no power base of abiding knowledge from which to operate as a source of interpretive wisdom?

As a physician I came out of a rigorous positivist tradition. In my orthopedic surgical residency, X-rays and physical examinations told us all we needed to know about the patient beyond the presenting complaint. I was torn from this comfortable professional life by two compelling events which converged on me at an impressionable age. They both impinged on my omnipotence and grandiosity from opposite directions, forcing me, like a sailboat between wind and water, onto a new course. The first was the agonizing death of a young man under my care following a motorcycle accident in which all the bones in his limbs were fractured and irreversibly infected. I had not realized that the attending surgeons chose not to see him during their rounds because they knew that it was hopeless. They left it to the most junior house officer, me, to handle.

Alan Kindler is a Training and Supervising Analyst with the Toronto Institute of Psychoanalysis. He is an Assistant Professor of Psychiatry in the University of Toronto Faculty of Medicine and a staff psychiatrist at St Michael's Hospital, Toronto.

© 1993 The Analytic Press

responseSet.title: On the “Mere” Fictions of Psychoanalysis: Reply to Sass
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the “Mere” Fictions of Psychoanalysis: Reply to Sass

Richard Geha PHD

Richard Geha

1224 How Lane
North Brunswick, NJ 08902

Richard Geha  

When nietzsche's (1887) perspicacious Madman rushed into the marketplace and accused humankind of having killed God, the people stood astonished and uncomprehending, neither able to recognize the act of deicide as their own nor able to apprehend the vastness of its terrible implications. Amid their stricken disbelief, the Madman wonders how, after this momentous murder, we can ever procure consolation. “Is not the greatness of this deed too great for us?” he asks. “Must we ourselves not become gods simply to appear worthy of it?” (p. 181). Elsewhere Nietzsche (1883) writes, “God is a conjecture; but I desire that your conjectures should not reach beyond your creative will” (p. 197). This forecasts our themes.

Paul Ricoeur (1970) names Marx, Nietzsche, and Freud “the three masters of suspicion”—each having deepened the doubts of our Cartesian heritage, each having mercilessly demystified the sacred. One major aftermath of that demystification has been the antirealist thought that has culminated in so much poststructuralist expression. Among other results, this antirealism has destabilized our commonsense notions of reality. Especially it has assailed those notions as they have been incorporated in the Myth of the Given (West, 1981) with all its attendant incarnations of what Derrida depicts as the logocentric dream of presence and plentitude that has possessed the mind of Western metaphysics. In art, this dismantlement of realist reality has furthered what Heller (1959) traces as “the Artist's Journey into the Interior.”

Out of that immemorial wish for presence have derived all the stabilities of our manifest, commonsense world—that world that Nietzsche (1887) pronounces a fable. I approached transference from that perspective, as an attempt to reinstate a realist metaphysics of presence. As Freud (1912) asserted, during an analysis we cannot slay anybody “in absentia” (p. 108). And yet, within the crucible of Freud's hermeneutics, the substantiality of such a presence simply dissipates.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis as “Conversation” and as “Fiction”: Commentary on Charles Spezzano's “A Relational Model of Inquiry and Truth” and Richard Geha's “Transferred Fictions”

Louis Sass PHD

Louis Sass

Piscataway NJ 08855

Louis Sass  

Both these papers involve admirable attempts to bring philosophical ideas to bear upon fundamental questions about the status of knowledge and interpretation in psychoanalysis. They both, however, strike me as deeply flawed — interestingly enough, for a similar reason: whereas each author professes to adopt a position of ultimate epistemological openness, what we get in each case is a profoundly leveling or homogenizing perspective on knowing, one that would reduce different forms of inquiry to an ultimately trivializing common denominator — “conversation” in Spezzano's case, “fiction” in that of Geha. To adopt the epistemological position of either of these writers would, in my opinion, be to forego the very possibility of making certain crucial distinctions that are demanded by common sense as well as by the requirements of responsible cognitive inquiry (whether the latter is conceived in an empiricist or a more hermeneutical fashion).

Let me begin with Spezzano's paper. Before addressing myself to the specific criticisms he offers of the article written by Robert Woolfolk and me, I would first like to consider two other features of his contribution.

Spezzano begins his argument with a discussion and critique of what he calls the “monadic” view of inquiry, discovery, and truth, which he goes on to contrast with his preferred “relational” view.

Dr. Sass is an Associate Professor of Clinical Psychology at Rutgers University. He is the author of Madness and Modernism: Insanity in the Light of Modern Art, Literature, and Thought and coeditor of Hermeneutics and Psychological Theory.

An Interview with Christopher Bollas

PD: In The Shadow of the Object you use the concept of the “transformational object” to extend Winnicott's contributions of the “holding environment” from a generic, “good-enough mothering” to an emphasis on the particularities of the individual mother. In Winnicott, the environment seems general and nonspecific, defined precisely in terms of the mother's ability to suspend her own subjectivity and mold a world around the baby. In your treatment, the distinct and specific features of the mother, her rhythms, her moods, the particularities of her “handling” are all built into the child's early experience of the very structure and textures of self and the world. Are you not, then, describing a different type of maternal influence in an infant's life from the kind of presence Winnicott defined?

CB: Winnicott wrote that there were two mothers, what he termed the “environment mother” and the “object mother.” The environment mother is, in effect, the holding environment, and this mother will be appreciated, if one can put it that way, by her unobtrusive support of the infant's developing subjectivity. Within this environment the infant will just be and over time develop a sense of his or her own being. It is less a matter of what the mother does than what she does not do. In a sense, not doing is the positive side of maternal holding, if we think of the primary task as not impinging and simply being with the infant. At the same time, however, the other mother presents objects to the baby, and we might say that the most important “object” she presents is herself.

PD: When you mention the other mother, does it make sense to you to refer to Lacan at this point?

responseSet.title: Action, Insight, and Working Through Outlines of an Integrative Approach
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Action, Insight, and Working Through Outlines of an Integrative Approach

Kenneth Frank PHD

Kenneth Frank

330 West 58th Street, Suite 200
New York, NY 10019

Kenneth Frank  

Action and insight form an essential dialectic within the psychoanalytic process. Yet many recent psychoanalytic formulations, while emphasizing integration, have continued to view psychoanalytic and action-oriented techniques dichotomously. It is reasoned that certain behavioral-cognitive techniques that promote adaptive behavior can become an active part of an in-depth analytic experience, providing material for an exploration that results in new ways not only of relating but also of experiencing the self and others. Such an approach, while at times helpful to certain patients, can be counterproductive for others. An integrative approach is compatible with many recent psychoanalytic frameworks emphasizing a two-person psychology, but not with a one-person vision of the psychoanalytic situation; in the two-person view, it is reasoned that an integrative approach is capable of maintaining the integrity of the analytic process. The outlined technique reflects a relational psychoanalytic orientation emphasizing internalized object relations, with the use of action-oriented techniques organized by the superordinate goal of modifying psychic structure, conceptualized principally in terms of self- and object-representations. Reasoning that this approach is congruent with certain theoretical contributions from Gill, Mitchell, Sandler, and especially Wachtel, among others, I state underlying assumptions of the approach, preliminary technical guidelines, and illustrative case material.

Dr. Frank is Director of Training, National Institute for the Psychotherapies, and Associate Clinical Professor (part-time) and senior staff member, Behavioral Medicine Program, Department of Psychiatry, Columbia University College of Physicians and Surgeons. He practices in New York City and Englewood, NJ.

I wish to thank Dr. Richard L. Rubens for his thoughtful reactions to parts of this manuscript.

[[RunningHead]]

Learning from the Patient

by Patrick Casement (New York: Guilford Press, xiv + 386 pp., $35.00)

Anthony Bass PHD

Anthony Bass

27 West 72nd Street, Suite 808
New York, NY 10023

Anthony Bass  

As psychoanalysts, we learn from our patients. We learn about how to do the work (technique), and we learn about ourselves. Such learning grows out of our direct and immediate experience with our patients. We understand that these two sorts of learning are really integrally linked, two dimensions of a single process, because the analyst works from the center of his own self-awareness. This informs his listening capabilities and perspective and therefore virtually all of his interventions. Limitations, obstacles, or constrictions in the analyst's awareness in relation to a given patient may lead to impasse, stalemate, or “negative therapeutic reaction,” and the fate of the analysis is likely to rest as much in the analyst's personal psychic resources for working through his own counterresistance, counteranxiety, and countertransference as in the patient's capability to work through resistance, anxiety, and transference. This working through on the analyst's side has been traditionally understood as taking place in the analyst's own psychoanalysis or self-analysis, but it may also take place directly or indirectly with the patient in the course of an analysis, and the patient may contribute to it. This process represents an aspect of the analyst's learning from the patient.

Learning from the Patient brings together Patrick Casement's two earlier books on the theme, Learning from the Patient (1985) and Further Learning from the Patient (1990).

Dr. Bass is an adjunct associate professor and clinical supervisor in the Clinical Psychology Doctoral Program at Teachers College, Columbia University. He is also on the supervising faculty at the National Institute for the Psychotherapies and on the teaching faculty at the Institute for Contemporary Psychotherapy.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentaries on Trop and Stolorow: Introduction

Stephen Mitchell PHD

Stephen Mitchell

251 W. 71st Street
New York, NY 10023

Stephen Mitchell  

One of the most hallowed ideals of most analytic clinicians is the principle of nondirectiveness. For many of us, what seems most precious and unique about the analytic process is its function as a vehicle for locating, developing, and protecting the genuine individuality and subjectivity of the patient, his or her true nature, his or her true self.

The psychoanalytic literature on homosexuality that emerged in the United States in the 1960s and 1970s reflected another ideal, which conflicted with the principle of nondirectiveness, an omnipotent ideal that placed the analyst in the position of arbiter of the good life. Homophobic currents within American culture merged with certain lines of psychoanalytic theory to produce the view that heterosexuality is the acme of emotional development and that homosexuality is, by definition, pathological. Within that view, analytic clinicians must insist that homosexual patients renounce their sexual orientation and actively direct the process of conversion into the “good life.” It is a reflection of the passion and fear of those who shaped this position that it seems to have gone unnoticed how far it took us from Freud's own views on sexual orientation (he considered it largely constitutional and not a proper focus for analytic treatment) and the central analytic ideal of nondirectiveness.

The directive-suggestive approach to sexual orientation and the clinical practice it shaped have been largely (although not universally) discredited and now seem, to many of us, a dark episode in American psychoanalysis. For the many patients who were subjected to this treatment approach, it caused considerable pain and contributed to a massive interference in their pursuit of their own personal meaning and satisfaction. From our current historical vantage point, it has seemed important and, perhaps, not overly difficult to rescue the ideal of nondirectiveness from the contaminating influences of homophobia, the medical model, and illegitimate, omnipotent moral claims to mental health and the good life.
